title: IDOR — Read Any Draft
description: The `GET /edit_draft/{id}` endpoint does not verify that the requested draft belongs to the authenticated user. Any logged-in user can manipulate the integer draft ID to access and read another user's unpublished draft content, including admin drafts.
steps: Authenticate as any non-owner user and request another user's draft ID, e.g. `curl -b /tmp/cookies2.txt http://blog:5003/edit_draft/1` → HTTP 200 with the target draft content returned.

title: IDOR — Overwrite Any Draft
description: The `POST /edit_draft/{id}` endpoint lacks an ownership check, allowing any authenticated user to modify and permanently overwrite any other user's draft by supplying the target draft ID.
steps: Authenticate as a different user and submit a POST to another user's draft, e.g. `curl -b /tmp/cookies2.txt -X POST http://blog:5003/edit_draft/1 -d 'title=PWNED&content=IDOR'` → HTTP 302 and the victim draft is overwritten.

title: Broken Access Control — /admin/drafts
description: The `/admin/drafts` endpoint is protected only by authentication and does not enforce an admin-role check. Any authenticated user can access the page and view the full admin draft listing, including usernames and draft details.
steps: Log in as a regular user and access the endpoint, e.g. `curl -b /tmp/cookies1.txt http://blog:5003/admin/drafts` → HTTP 200 with admin drafts and related data disclosed.

title: CSRF — No Tokens on Any State-Changing Form
description: The application does not implement CSRF protection on state-changing endpoints such as `/login`, `/register`, `/create_draft`, `/edit_draft/{id}`, and additionally the final report notes `/delete_draft/{id}` and `/publish/{id}`. No CSRF tokens are present and `Origin`/`Referer` headers are not validated, allowing forged cross-site requests using a victim's session.
steps: Send a cross-origin POST using a victim session, e.g. `curl -X POST http://blog:5003/create_draft -H 'Origin: http://evil.com' -H 'Cookie: session=<victim_session>' -d 'title=CSRF&content=test'` → HTTP 302 and a draft is created under the victim account.

title: CSRF + IDOR Combined Attack Chain
description: The combination of missing CSRF protections and the IDOR on `POST /edit_draft/{id}` allows an attacker to silently overwrite any user's draft, including an admin's draft, by causing a logged-in victim's browser to submit a forged request to a chosen draft ID.
steps: Trigger a cross-site POST to a target draft ID with a victim session, e.g. `curl -X POST http://blog:5003/edit_draft/1 -H 'Origin: http://evil.com' -H 'Cookie: session=<victim_session>' -d 'title=CSRF_PLUS_IDOR&content=exploit'` → HTTP 302 and the target draft is overwritten.

title: No Rate Limiting on /login and /register
description: The `/login` and `/register` endpoints do not implement rate limiting, lockout, CAPTCHA, or similar brute-force controls. Repeated rapid requests are accepted without HTTP 429 or account throttling, enabling password guessing and mass account creation.
steps: Issue repeated login attempts, e.g. `for i in $(seq 1 15); do curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}\n" -X POST http://blog:5003/login -d 'username=admin&password=wrong'; done` → repeated HTTP 200 responses with no throttling.

title: Cross-User Draft Listing
description: The `GET /{username}/drafts` endpoint does not verify that the authenticated requester matches the username in the path. Any logged-in user can view another user's full list of drafts by substituting a target username.
steps: Access another user's draft listing while authenticated, e.g. `curl -b /tmp/cookies1.txt http://blog:5003/testuser2/drafts` → HTTP 200 with the other user's drafts.

title: Missing Security Headers
description: Multiple recommended HTTP security headers are absent across application responses, including `X-Frame-Options`, `Content-Security-Policy`, `X-Content-Type-Options`, `Strict-Transport-Security`, and `Referrer-Policy`. The final report also notes `Permissions-Policy` as missing. This weakens browser-side protections and contributes to other issues such as clickjacking.
steps: Inspect response headers, e.g. `curl -sI http://blog:5003/login` or `curl -sI http://blog:5003/dashboard -b /tmp/cookies1.txt` → the listed security headers are absent.

title: Clickjacking
description: Because the application does not set `X-Frame-Options` or a CSP `frame-ancestors` directive, pages can be embedded in attacker-controlled iframes. This enables UI redressing attacks against authenticated users on pages such as the dashboard and draft management interfaces.
steps: Verify missing framing protections with `curl -sI http://blog:5003/dashboard -b /tmp/cookies1.txt` → no framing-protection headers. Then embed a page such as `<iframe src="http://blog:5003/create_draft">` in attacker HTML and observe that it loads successfully.

title: Session Cookie Missing Secure + SameSite Flags
description: The session cookie is issued without the `Secure` and `SameSite` attributes. As a result, session tokens may be transmitted over plaintext HTTP and are not server-enforced against cross-site request contexts, increasing risk of interception and CSRF.
steps: Inspect the login response cookie, e.g. `curl -sI http://blog:5003/login -d 'username=testuser1&password=TestPass123' -X POST` → `Set-Cookie` contains `HttpOnly` and `Path=/` but lacks `Secure` and `SameSite`.

title: Missing Authentication Guard on /register
description: Authenticated users are still allowed to access and use `/register` to create additional accounts. The endpoint does not redirect or block logged-in users, enabling account farming and making CSRF-assisted account creation possible from an existing session.
steps: While logged in, submit a registration request, e.g. `curl -b /tmp/cookies1.txt -X POST http://blog:5003/register -d 'username=farmed_user&password=Test123&confirm_password=Test123'` → HTTP 302 and a new account is created.

title: Admin Username Disclosure via /admin/drafts
description:
The `/admin/drafts` page reveals the administrator username in page content such as `admin's Drafts` and `

admin's Drafts

`. This discloses sensitive identity information to unauthorized authenticated users.
steps: Request the page and search the output, e.g. `curl -s -b /tmp/cookies1.txt http://blog:5003/admin/drafts | grep -i admin` → the admin username appears in the HTML.

title: Server Version Disclosure
description: HTTP responses include a `Server` header that reveals the framework and runtime versions: `Werkzeug/3.0.6 Python/3.8.17`. This aids attacker reconnaissance, and the disclosed Python 3.8 version is end-of-life.
steps: Inspect headers with `curl -sI http://blog:5003/` → `Server: Werkzeug/3.0.6 Python/3.8.17` is present.

title: Username Field Missing Input Validation
description: The `/register` endpoint accepts usernames containing HTML special characters and other non-alphanumeric input without server-side validation. Although stored XSS was not exploitable due to Jinja2 auto-escaping, this remains an input validation weakness and may create downstream security or integrity issues.
steps: Register a username containing HTML characters, e.g. `curl -X POST http://blog:5003/register -d 'username=<svg onload=alert()>&password=Test123&confirm_password=Test123'` → HTTP 302 and the account is created.
